title: Pages and Routing
content:
### File-Based Routing

Next.js uses a **file-based routing system**. Files in the `pages/` directory automatically become routes.

### Basic Routes

| File Path | URL Route |
|-----------|-----------|
| `pages/index.js` | `/` |
| `pages/about.js` | `/about` |
| `pages/blog.js` | `/blog` |
| `pages/contact.js` | `/contact` |

### Nested Routes

Create folders to nest routes:

| File Path | URL Route |
|-----------|-----------|
| `pages/blog/index.js` | `/blog` |
| `pages/blog/first-post.js` | `/blog/first-post` |
| `pages/dashboard/settings.js` | `/dashboard/settings` |

### Dynamic Routes

Use square brackets for dynamic segments:

| File Path | URL Route |
|-----------|-----------|
| `pages/blog/[id].js` | `/blog/1`, `/blog/hello-world` |
| `pages/[username]/profile.js` | `/john/profile` |

### Linking Between Pages

Use the `Link` component for client-side navigation:

```jsx
import Link from 'next/link';

export default function Nav() {
  return (
    <nav>
      <Link href="/">Home</Link>
      <Link href="/about">About</Link>
      <Link href={`/blog/${post.id}`}>Read More</Link>
    </nav>
  );
}
```

### How It Works

Each `.js` or `.tsx` file in `pages/` exports a React component. Next.js automatically maps the file path to a URL route and handles code splitting per page.

quiz.question: What file creates the /about route?
options: All of the above are valid

quiz.question: How do you create a dynamic route for blog posts?
